understand.plain: The function counts how many times you can right-shift 'num' before it becomes 0, which equals the number of bits needed to represent 'num' in binary. Each right-shift by 1 is equivalent to integer division by 2. For num = 430, we keep shifting right until the value reaches 0, counting each shift.

keywords.explain: The >>= 1 operator shifts all bits one position to the right, effectively dividing the integer by 2 (discarding remainder). The loop runs once per bit position in the binary representation.
keywords.example: num = 6 (binary 110): shift gives 3 (011), then 1 (001), then 0 — count = 3 = number of bits in 6

keywords.explain: The number of bits required to represent a positive integer n in binary is floor(log_2(n)) + 1. The function computes exactly this.
keywords.example: 7 in binary is 111 (3 bits), 8 in binary is 1000 (4 bits)

understand.visual_alt: Table showing 430 being right-shifted step by step: 430 -> 215 -> 107 -> 53 -> 26 -> 13 -> 6 -> 3 -> 1 -> 0, counting 9 shifts total. 430 in binary is 110101110 (9 bits).

given.aim: Find the value returned by func(430) where the function counts right-shifts until num becomes 0.

terms.meaning: The input integer to the function
terms.connects: We right-shift 430 repeatedly until it becomes 0

terms.meaning: Incremented once for every iteration of the while loop (i.e., once per right-shift)
terms.example: If num=7 (111 binary), count reaches 3 after three shifts
terms.connects: The return value is equal to the number of bits in the binary representation of num

terms.meaning: Right-shift num by 1 bit position each iteration, equivalent to floor(num/2)
terms.connects: The loop runs exactly floor(log_2(num)) + 1 times for any positive num

given.plan: Convert 430 to binary to count bits directly, or simulate the loop: 430 -> 215 -> 107 -> 53 -> 26 -> 13 -> 6 -> 3 -> 1 -> 0 (9 shifts). Alternatively, floor(log_2(430)) + 1 = floor(8.75) + 1 = 8 + 1 = 9.

steps.title: Convert 430 to binary
steps.note: The binary representation has 9 bits, so the function will loop exactly 9 times.

steps.title: Simulate the while loop
steps.apply:
Iteration 1: num=430 (nonzero) -> count=1, num=215
Iteration 2: num=215 (nonzero) -> count=2, num=107
Iteration 3: num=107 (nonzero) -> count=3, num=53
Iteration 4: num=53  (nonzero) -> count=4, num=26
Iteration 5: num=26  (nonzero) -> count=5, num=13
Iteration 6: num=13  (nonzero) -> count=6, num=6
Iteration 7: num=6   (nonzero) -> count=7, num=3
Iteration 8: num=3   (nonzero) -> count=8, num=1
Iteration 9: num=1   (nonzero) -> count=9, num=0
Loop ends (num=0). Return count = 9.
steps.note: func(num) computes the bit-length of num. For num=430, it returns 9.

questions.formula_note: The function computes floor(log_2(num)) + 1, the number of bits in the binary representation of num. Each right-shift >>= 1 divides num by 2; the loop count equals the bit length.

questions.to_find: What the pseudocode outputs after processing matrix A

understand.plain: The pseudocode looks like a matrix transpose, but both (i,j) and (j,i) pairs are swapped twice — once when i<j and once when i>j. Because the double nested loop iterates over ALL (i,j) pairs including both (i,j) and (j,i), every swap is undone. The final matrix A is the same as the original.

keywords.explain: A transpose swaps element at position (i,j) with the element at (j,i). Correct in-place transpose only swaps when i < j to avoid double-swapping.
keywords.example: If A[1][2]=5 and A[2][1]=8, after transpose A[1][2]=8, A[2][1]=5

keywords.explain: When the loop iterates over all (i,j) including both (1,2) and (2,1), the swap is applied twice to each pair, returning them to their original values.
keywords.example: Swap A[1][2] with A[2][1]: 5<->8 gives A[1][2]=8, A[2][1]=5. Then when i=2,j=1 we swap again: 8<->5 gives original values.

understand.visual_alt: For a 2x2 matrix with elements a, b, c, d: when i=1,j=2 we swap b and c (getting c,b). When i=2,j=1 we swap them again (getting b,c back). Final matrix is identical to the original.

given.aim: Determine what the pseudocode computes and what it outputs for matrix A.

terms.meaning: A constant used in the swap to temporarily hold sum; it is added and then subtracted so its net effect is zero.
terms.example: Temp = A[i][j] + 100; A[j][i] = Temp - 100 = A[i][j]. This is just a standard swap using addition/subtraction.
terms.connects: C does not change the values, it only acts as a temporary carrier for the swap operation

terms.meaning: Both i and j range from 1 to n, so every cell (i,j) is visited including (i,j) and (j,i) for every pair i != j.
terms.example: For n=2: loop visits (1,1),(1,2),(2,1),(2,2) — includes both (1,2) and (2,1)
terms.connects: Since both (i,j) and (j,i) are visited, each off-diagonal swap happens twice, undoing itself

terms.meaning: Temp=A[i][j]+C; A[i][j]=A[j][i]; A[j][i]=Temp-C; effectively swaps A[i][j] and A[j][i].
terms.connects: When the same pair is swapped again at (j,i), the values return to original

given.plan: Trace the swap for a specific off-diagonal pair (i,j) and then for (j,i). Show that applying the swap twice restores the original values. Diagonal elements (i=j) are swapped with themselves, which is a no-op. Conclusion: the output is the original matrix A.

steps.title: Analyze the swap operation and the role of constant C
steps.formula_raw: Temp = A[i][j] + C; A[i][j] = A[j][i]; A[j][i] = Temp - C
steps.apply:
The three-line block swaps A[i][j] and A[j][i]:
  Temp = A[i][j] + C  (save A[i][j] in Temp, offset by C)
  A[i][j] = A[j][i]  (overwrite A[i][j] with A[j][i])
  A[j][i] = Temp - C = (A[i][j]_old + C) - C = A[i][j]_old  (restore original A[i][j] into A[j][i])
This is a valid swap. C is a red herring — it cancels out.
steps.note: The swap is correct and equivalent to the standard 3-variable swap using a Temp variable.

steps.title: Identify that every off-diagonal swap is performed twice
steps.formula_raw: For all i in [1,n], j in [1,n]: swap(A[i][j], A[j][i])
steps.apply:
Consider any pair (r, s) with r != s:
- When i=r, j=s: swap(A[r][s], A[s][r]) is performed. Let original values be A[r][s]=x, A[s][r]=y. After: A[r][s]=y, A[s][r]=x.
- When i=s, j=r: swap(A[s][r], A[r][s]) is performed again. After: A[s][r]=x, A[r][s]=y — back to original!
For diagonal (i=j): swap(A[i][i], A[i][i]) — no change.
Conclusion: After all iterations, every element is back to its original value.
steps.note: A correct in-place transpose would iterate only over i < j (upper triangle). By iterating over all (i,j), every swap is undone.

steps.title: Verify with a concrete 2x2 example
steps.note: The output is exactly the original matrix A, not its transpose.

solution.result: The output is the matrix A itself (Option A). The double-swap over all (i,j) pairs cancels every transposition, leaving A unchanged.

questions.formula_note: In-place matrix transpose correctness: swapping A[i][j] with A[j][i] for all (i,j) — not just i < j — performs each swap twice, which is equivalent to the identity transformation. The constant C is a distractor that cancels out algebraically.
